description: A realistic undergraduate economics game theory sample exam for system testing.

questions.stem: In a normal-form game, what is a dominant strategy?

options.text: A strategy that gives the highest payoff only when the opponent cooperates
A strategy that yields at least as high a payoff as any other strategy for every action of the opponent
A strategy that is chosen with positive probability in mixed equilibrium
A strategy that maximizes total welfare for all players

questions.explanation: A dominant strategy is best regardless of what the other player does. It is defined player by player, not by total welfare.

questions.stem: Which statement best describes a Nash equilibrium?

options.text: Each player is maximizing joint payoffs
Each player is using a dominant strategy
No player can improve their payoff by deviating unilaterally
The government has eliminated strategic uncertainty

questions.explanation: A Nash equilibrium is a profile of strategies where each strategy is a best response to the others, so no one gains by changing alone.

questions.stem: Which of the following are standard assumptions of a one-shot Prisoner's Dilemma?

options.text: Each prisoner chooses simultaneously
Mutual confession yields a lower payoff than unilateral silence against confession
The game is repeated an infinite number of times
Each player takes the other player's action as given when choosing

questions.explanation: The one-shot Prisoner's Dilemma is simultaneous and strategic. Infinite repetition is not part of the one-shot setup.

questions.stem: In a Cournot duopoly, firms compete primarily by choosing:

options.text: Prices
Advertising intensity
Quantities
Product quality standards

questions.explanation: The canonical Cournot model treats quantities as strategic choices made simultaneously, with price determined by market demand.

questions.stem: Compared with Cournot competition, Bertrand competition with homogeneous goods and constant marginal cost usually predicts:

options.text: A higher price and lower output
Price equal to marginal cost
Collusion as the unique equilibrium
No pure-strategy equilibrium

questions.explanation: Under standard Bertrand assumptions, price competition drives price down to marginal cost, producing the Bertrand paradox.

questions.stem: Which statements about subgame perfect equilibrium are correct?

options.text: It rules out non-credible threats in dynamic games
Every subgame perfect equilibrium is also a Nash equilibrium
It only applies to simultaneous-move games
Backward induction is often used to find it in finite perfect-information games

questions.explanation: Subgame perfection refines Nash equilibrium in dynamic settings by requiring optimal play in every subgame, and backward induction is the standard tool in finite perfect-information cases.

questions.stem: A sequential entry game in which the incumbent threatens a price war after entry illustrates the importance of:

options.text: Pareto efficiency
Credibility of off-path actions
Comparative advantage
Revealed preference

questions.explanation: Entry deterrence examples often turn on whether a threat is credible once the entrant has already entered.

questions.stem: What concept describes a player's strategy being optimal given beliefs formed after observing previous moves in a dynamic game with incomplete information?
questions.explanation: Perfect Bayesian equilibrium combines sequential rationality with belief updating, making it a central solution concept in dynamic games with incomplete information.

questions.stem: If two players randomize in equilibrium so that each makes the other indifferent across pure strategies, the equilibrium is:

options.text: A correlated equilibrium
A mixed-strategy Nash equilibrium
A dominant-strategy equilibrium
A cooperative bargaining solution

questions.explanation: The indifference condition is the standard way to compute a mixed-strategy Nash equilibrium.

questions.stem: Which factors can help sustain collusion in an infinitely repeated oligopoly?

options.text: A high discount factor
Frequent interaction
Immediate detection of deviation
One firm having strictly dominant deviation profits with no future punishment

questions.explanation: Collusion is easier to sustain when firms value the future, meet often, and can detect deviations quickly. If deviation brings gains with no punishment, collusion collapses.

questions.stem: In a signaling model, a separating equilibrium means that:

options.text: All types choose the same signal
Signals carry no information
Different types choose different signals, allowing receivers to infer type
Receivers ignore the signal and randomize uniformly

questions.explanation: A separating equilibrium reveals private information because different sender types choose distinguishable signals.

questions.stem: Name the refinement of Nash equilibrium that requires players to assign beliefs using Bayes' rule wherever possible after observing a signal.
questions.explanation: The defining idea is that strategies must be sequentially rational and beliefs should be updated by Bayes' rule whenever the observed history occurs with positive probability.
